title: The practice of self-compassion when it feels completely fake
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
When self-compassion feels like a lie

I remember the first time someone told me to be kind to myself. I was sitting in a therapist's office, crying about something I couldn't even name anymore. And she said it so gently, so earnestly. "Try to have some compassion for yourself."

I wanted to throw something at her.

Not because she was wrong. Not because I didn't want to feel better. But because the suggestion felt like a betrayal of everything I knew to be true about myself. Self-compassion wasn't just uncomfortable. It felt dangerous. It felt like letting my guard down. It felt like pretending the monster inside me didn't exist.

Here's the thing. When you've spent years or decades surviving in a body that learned to equate self-criticism with safety, self-compassion doesn't feel like relief. It feels like a trap. Your nervous system reads kindness as a threat. It says, "What are you doing? We don't do that here. That's how we get hurt."

So if you're reading this and self-compassion feels fake, hollow, or even repulsive, you're not broken. You're not doing it wrong. You're experiencing a predictable response from a nervous system that learned to survive by keeping you small, vigilant, and critical.

Stay with me here. Because I think there's another way to approach this. A way that doesn't require you to pretend you're okay when you're not.

The nervous system's objection to kindness

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to cues of safety and danger. What he found is that your body is constantly scanning for signals, even when your conscious mind isn't paying attention. It's looking for evidence that you're safe. And when you've lived through trauma, your nervous system gets really good at spotting danger. It gets really bad at spotting safety.

So when you try to offer yourself kindness, your body might interpret that as a threat. It might say, "Wait. We let our guard down once before. Remember what happened?" And then it floods you with shame, or numbness, or that hollow feeling of "this doesn't work for me."

Recovery isn't linear. I know you've heard that before. But I want to say it differently. Recovery isn't just "not linear" - it's actively confusing. You'll have a week where everything feels possible and then a day where you can't get out of bed. You'll think you've healed something and then it shows up again wearing a different costume. That's not failure. That's how it works.

Pete Walker talks about "emotional flashbacks" - those periods where you're suddenly flooded with the feelings of childhood without any visual memory attached. You just feel small, helpless, worthless, terrified - and you don't know why. These can last hours or days. And they can happen years into recovery. That doesn't mean you haven't made progress. It means there are layers. There are always more layers.

What I've learned is this. The goal isn't to never be triggered again. The goal is to recover faster. To recognize what's happening sooner. To have more tools available. To be gentler with yourself when you fall. That's progress. Not perfection. Not the absence of pain. Just a different relationship with it. A relationship where you're not alone in it anymore.

That's not a failure of will. That's a failure of the conditions your body needs to feel safe enough to receive kindness.

I spent years trying to force myself to say nice things in the mirror. It felt like lying to a child about the monsters under the bed. The child knows the monsters are real. They've seen them. They've been bitten by them. Telling them "everything is fine" doesn't help. It just makes them feel crazy.

What helped was understanding that self-compassion isn't a feeling. It's a practice. And like any practice, it starts with where you actually are, not where you think you should be.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses the inner critic and why self-compassion feels so foreign when you've been conditioned to attack yourself as a survival strategy. He doesn't tell you to just "be nice." He explains the mechanics of why you can't.

Start with the body, not the affirmations

Here's where I think the wellness industry gets it wrong. They tell you to repeat mantras. They tell you to write in a gratitude journal. They tell you to "speak to yourself like you would a friend." And all of that might work for someone whose nervous system hasn't been shaped by complex trauma. But for many of us, it doesn't land. It feels like putting a Band-Aid on a bullet wound.

So I want to suggest something different. Something that might feel less fake.

Start with your body. Not your thoughts. Not your beliefs. Not your inner monologue. Just your physical sensations.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story you tell yourself. It's a posture, a tension pattern, a way of holding your breath. And you can't think your way out of a body-based survival response. You have to work with the body directly.

So try this. Don't tell yourself "I love myself." That might feel like a lie. Instead, put your hand on your chest. Or your stomach. Or wherever feels neutral. Just rest it there. Don't say anything. Don't try to feel anything. Just notice what happens.

Maybe your body relaxes a little. Maybe it tenses up more. Maybe you feel nothing at all. All of that is data. All of that is your nervous system telling you where it's at.

Right? That's already different from forcing an affirmation you don't believe.

From there, you can experiment with small things. A gentle exhale. A slow blink. A shift in your posture that feels slightly less defended. These are micro-movements toward self-compassion that don't require you to believe anything. They just require you to be curious about what your body is doing.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She frames self-compassion as a kind of radical permission to be exactly where you are, without having to fix it first. That might sound soft. But it's actually one of the hardest things you'll ever do.

I want to end with something that might sound strange. Gratitude for your symptoms. Not toxic positivity gratitude. Not "everything happens for a reason" gratitude. Real gratitude. For the parts of you that kept you alive. For the hypervigilance that spotted real danger. For the dissociation that protected you from unbearable pain. For the fawn response that kept you safe when fighting back wasn't an option.

Richard Schwartz says that when we approach our protector parts with gratitude instead of frustration, something shifts. They soften. They don't need to work so hard. Because they're finally being seen for what they are - not enemies, not pathology, but loyal soldiers who never got the message that the war is over.

So here's my invitation. The next time a symptom shows up - the anxiety, the numbness, the rage, the people-pleasing - before you try to fix it or fight it or shame yourself for it, try this. Say "thank you." Say "I know you're trying to help." Say "I see you." And then, gently, ask: "Is there something else you need me to know?" You might be surprised by the answer. You might be surprised by what becomes possible when you stop fighting yourself and start listening instead.

The parts that hate self-compassion

Richard Schwartz developed Internal Family Systems therapy, and his model changed how I understand this whole problem. He says that our minds are made up of different parts, different voices, each with its own job and its own perspective. And some of those parts are really, really opposed to self-compassion.

There might be a part of you that says self-compassion is weak. That part learned that vulnerability gets you hurt, and it's trying to protect you. There might be a part that says you don't deserve kindness. That part might be carrying shame that doesn't even belong to you. There might be a part that just feels disgusted by the whole idea. That part might be so used to self-attack that kindness feels like a betrayal of your survival strategy.

Here's what I've learned. You don't have to convince those parts to go away. You don't have to override them. You can just acknowledge them. You can say, "I see that part of me that thinks this is stupid. I see that part of me that feels disgusted. I'm not going to argue with you. I'm just going to notice you're here."

That's self-compassion, by the way. Not the kind that feels good. The kind that's honest. The kind that includes the parts that hate self-compassion.

Think about it. If you can be curious about the resistance instead of fighting it, you're already practicing something. You're already turning toward yourself with attention. That's the seed. That's the beginning.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It walks through the idea that every part of you, even the ones that seem cruel or self-destructive, is trying to protect you in some way. When you understand that, self-compassion stops being about forcing kindness and starts being about understanding the logic of your own survival system.

What to do when it still feels fake

I'm not going to tell you that if you just try hard enough, self-compassion will eventually feel natural. For some of us, it never feels fully natural. And that's okay. You don't have to like it. You don't have to believe it. You just have to keep the practice, even when it feels like nothing is happening.

Because here's what I've noticed. Over time, the resistance softens. Not because you convinced yourself you deserve kindness. But because your nervous system starts to learn, through repeated small experiences, that being kind to yourself doesn't kill you. It doesn't make you weak. It doesn't invite disaster.

It just makes you a person who is trying to live with a little less suffering.

And that's not fake. That's not a lie. That's just a practice that takes longer than anyone wants to admit.

Wild, right? The thing that's supposed to heal you feels like the biggest threat. But that's exactly why it's worth doing. Not because it's easy. Because it's honest. Because it meets you where you are, even if where you are is a place where self-compassion feels completely fake.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you," which is a form of self-compassion that doesn't require you to feel warm and fuzzy. It just requires you to be curious about your own history.

So I'll leave you with this. You don't have to love yourself. You don't have to believe you're worthy. You don't have to feel anything warm or fuzzy. You just have to keep showing up, with your hand on your chest, with your resistance, with your disgust, with your skepticism. And that's enough. That's the practice. That's the return.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
